_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 7-3 Crime Scene Management (Revised 1/5/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Procedures Protocol Section 7-3: Crime Scene Management — MDHHS Approved 1/5/2023
purpose: EMS providers may respond to scenes that are or become crime scenes. The first duty is emergency medical care. EMS must minimize disturbance to potential evidence while fulfilling patient care responsibilities, and must document and communicate any alterations made to the scene.

sections.title: General Principles
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 7-3: Crime Scene Management — General Principles
points: First duty is emergency medical care — scene safety and patient care take priority over evidence preservation
Attempt to preserve evidence without compromising patient care
Notify law enforcement of all alterations made to the scene as a result of patient care
Document all alterations in the patient care report (PCR)

sections.title: Evidence Preservation Practices
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 7-3: Crime Scene Management — Evidence Preservation
points: NEVER cut through bullet holes or knife holes/tears in clothing — cut around them
Retain cut clothing and place in PAPER bags (not plastic — prevents moisture and DNA degradation); give to law enforcement
Do not disturb outdoor evidence when possible: shoe prints, tire marks, shell casings, blood patterns
Touch only what is necessary for patient care
Minimize the number of personnel on scene — limit foot traffic through the crime scene
Do not use crime scene sinks, toilets, or surfaces unless absolutely required for patient care
Do not move items unless required for patient access or care
Note and document the position of any items moved for patient care

sections.title: Patient and Witness Considerations
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 7-3: Crime Scene Management — Patient and Witness
points: Advise the patient not to shower, bathe, change clothing, or wash hands prior to examination at the hospital (especially in sexual assault and physical assault cases)
Document the patient's emotional state objectively
Document verbatim any spontaneous statements made by the patient or bystanders — use quotation marks
Do not interrogate the patient or witnesses — that is a law enforcement function
Document the identity and relationship of any individuals present at the scene

sections.title: Communication with Law Enforcement
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 7-3: Crime Scene Management — Law Enforcement Coordination
points: Notify law enforcement of all changes made to the scene as a result of patient care before leaving the scene
Inform law enforcement of: patient's position when found, items moved or touched, clothing removed or cut, medications administered, IV access established, any other interventions
If law enforcement is not on scene: notify dispatch and document in PCR
Cooperate with law enforcement while maintaining patient care responsibilities

deterioration_flags: Scene safety threat — withdraw to safe perimeter; do not enter until law enforcement clears scene
Patient requires rapid intervention — patient care supersedes evidence preservation; document alterations thoroughly
scope_notes: First duty is always emergency medical care — evidence preservation is secondary
All scene alterations must be communicated to law enforcement and documented in PCR
Clothing with bullet/knife holes: never cut through holes — cut around them; store in paper bags
